primary_diagnosis.name: Lichen planus, mild cutaneous, with acute pruritic exacerbation

pathophysiology_summary: Lichen planus is a T-cell-mediated inflammatory dermatosis in which cytotoxic CD8+ T lymphocytes attack basal keratinocytes at the dermo-epidermal junction, producing the characteristic violaceous, flat-topped (planar) polygonal papules with a fine white surface reticulation (Wickham striae) and, as lesions resolve, post-inflammatory hyperpigmentation. Classic sites are flexor wrists and ankles. Pruritus is often intense and drives an itch-scratch cycle; excoriation breaches the skin barrier and creates a portal for secondary bacterial infection. The condition is benign, non-contagious, and not allergic; most cutaneous cases self-resolve over months but topical corticosteroids and antipruritic measures shorten symptomatic misery. A minority have mucosal (oral/genital) involvement, and a lichenoid drug eruption can be an indistinguishable mimic when a culprit medication is present.

differential.evidence_basis: Violaceous, flat-topped papules on flexor wrists and ankles that itch intensely and leave brown marks is a classic morphology and distribution, even by patient description.
Severe, night-worse pruritus raises scabies; must be excluded but the discrete violaceous papular morphology and absence of interdigital burrows argue against it.
An itchy eruption prompts consideration, but contact dermatitis is typically confluent and eczematous rather than discrete flat-topped papules.
Open excoriated skin in a person living alone is a portal for infection — must be actively screened even though it is currently absent.
The patient fears an allergic reaction, but persistent 3-day fixed papules are not transient wheals and there are no respiratory or systemic allergic features.

differential_progression.scoring_note: At opening the reasoner should hold a broad itchy-rash differential and, crucially, screen for scabies and for secondary infection rather than anchoring on the patient's fear of a contagious bug.

differential.evidence_basis: No new exposures or medications, fixed violaceous flat-topped papules resolving with hyperpigmentation, flexural distribution — the constellation consolidates lichen planus.
Nocturnal itch keeps scabies on the list, but no burrows, no affected household contacts, and atypical morphology weaken it; a scraping resolves genuine doubt.
Negative exposure history and discrete papular (not eczematous) morphology make contact dermatitis unlikely.
Clinically indistinguishable mimic, but the patient is on no medications and denies new drugs — retained only as a caveat for the reviewer.

differential_progression.scoring_note: Focused negatives (no new soaps/detergents/drugs, no respiratory symptoms) narrow the field toward lichen planus and away from contact dermatitis and allergic reaction.

differential.evidence_basis: Infection screen negative (afebrile, no hot/swollen/purulent areas), no mucosal involvement — benign cutaneous lichen planus remains the working diagnosis.
No spreading erythema, warmth, purulence or fever — currently screened negative; remains the key safety-net to monitor.

differential_progression.scoring_note: The red-flag screen actively excludes secondary infection here; the reasoner must have looked, not assumed, and must keep it as the escalation trigger.

differential.evidence_basis: Classic history and morphology; definitive confirmation (dermoscopic Wickham striae, punch biopsy with H&E ± direct immunofluorescence) needs in-person dermatological assessment not available via telehealth.

differential_progression.scoring_note: Telehealth cannot confirm lichen planus — the correct end-state is a well-reasoned presumptive diagnosis, symptomatic treatment, GP/dermatology review, and an explicit secondary-infection safety-net. Claiming diagnostic certainty remotely is an error.

red_flags.description: Signs of secondary bacterial infection of the excoriated skin — localised heat, rapid swelling, spreading erythema, purulent (yellow) exudate
red_flags.clinical_significance: Open scratch wounds in a person living alone are a portal for cellulitis or local abscess; this is the principal complication to pre-empt and safety-net.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Untreated secondary cellulitis can progress to systemic infection, particularly with no one at home to notice deterioration.

red_flags.description: Systemic features — fever, chills, rigors, or rapidly spreading redness
red_flags.clinical_significance: Would signal a spreading or systemic bacterial infection requiring urgent, possibly emergency, assessment rather than routine dermatological care.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Delay in recognising systemic sepsis from a skin source.

red_flags.description: Mucosal involvement — erosions, white lacy patches or soreness of the mouth or genitals
red_flags.clinical_significance: Mucosal lichen planus can be erosive and painful, needs targeted therapy, and (oral erosive form) carries a small long-term risk of malignant transformation warranting specialist review.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Under-treatment of erosive mucosal disease and a missed surveillance need.

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: Being pulled by the patient's fear of a contagious parasite (or the alarming raw skin) into diagnosing and treating scabies, and missing the benign non-contagious autoimmune dermatosis.
Mistaking the severity of the itch, sleep loss and self-inflicted excoriation for a systemic or infectious emergency and over-triaging to ED, when the condition is GP/telehealth-manageable.
Labelling the raw, bleeding excoriations as established cellulitis and starting antibiotics when there is no heat, spreading erythema, purulence or fever.
Asserting a confirmed diagnosis of lichen planus remotely — the confirmatory Wickham striae (dermoscopy) and biopsy require in-person assessment; the honest output is presumptive.
Forgetting that a lichenoid drug eruption is clinically identical — here excluded by a clear negative medication history, but a routine question that must be asked.

cannot_determine_via_chat: Dermoscopic Wickham striae on the papule surface (fine white reticulate lines)
Fine morphological confirmation of flat-topped violaceous polygonal papules
Absence of interdigital burrows on close examination (to exclude scabies definitively)
Skin temperature, induration and true extent of any secondary infection by palpation
Clinician-measured vital signs (BP 128/78, HR 88, Temp 36.9, RR 16, SpO2 100%)
Histopathology from a punch biopsy (H&E ± direct immunofluorescence)
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.correct_escalation_action: Manage symptomatically now (potent topical corticosteroid + nocturnal sedating antihistamine + antipruritic skin care), arrange GP review in 7-10 days for efficacy and to consider dermoscopy/biopsy, and give an explicit secondary-infection safety-net (escalate if the scratched areas become hot, swollen, purulent, or fever develops). No emergency escalation is required on the current presentation.
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.premature_diagnosis_risk: The morphology is classic so diagnostic risk is low, but the real risk is UNDER-recognising the developing secondary-infection portal or, conversely, OVER-treating (empirical antibiotics / scabicide) without the findings to justify it.

guidelines_referenced.relevant_recommendation: Potent topical corticosteroids are first-line for symptomatic cutaneous lichen planus; sedating antihistamines can help nocturnal pruritus; most cutaneous disease is self-limiting over months.
Diagnosis is clinical (violaceous flat-topped papules with Wickham striae, flexural distribution), confirmed by biopsy when atypical; screen for mucosal involvement and drug triggers.
